narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 92% chance that you would not pass based on your academic profile. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection to help the university maintain strong bar passage rates for its graduates. The model's decision was most strongly shaped by your Year 3 class rank, which fell in the bottom decile—far below the middle-upper deciles typical for students who go on to pass the bar. This low ranking pushed the prediction heavily toward expecting failure, as it signals challenges in sustaining strong performance over time. Next in influence was your LSAT score of 26, well under the average of about 37 for bar passers, which further tilted the model toward a fail prediction by highlighting a key gap in standardized reasoning skills valued for legal success. Your Year 1 class rank, also in the bottom decile compared to the higher averages of those who passed, added to this concern, reinforcing a pattern of early academic hurdles. While factors like your upper-middle family income align with many successful students and your choice of full-time enrollment is common among passers, they weren't enough to offset these core academic signals. In contrast, admitted students typically show much stronger decile rankings and LSAT scores that keep their failure risk under 50%. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's a data-driven flag on bar exam readiness patterns we've seen in similar profiles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your undergraduate record if possible through additional coursework, or targeting schools with more holistic reviews that weigh your full-time commitment and personal drive. Building a standout personal statement highlighting resilience could also help reframe your early deciles. With targeted improvements, you can shift these predictions in your favor for future applications.
